what: 1. c~l regression: circular response, linear covariate
title: circlss among circular-response regression packages
text: Fisher & Lee’s 31 periwinkles: direction moved (circular) distance moved (linear) — shared example circular NPCirc. circular: von Mises link mu = mu0 + 2*atan(b*x); location , one global concentration. NPCirc: nonparametric conditional-mean curve (Nadaraya–Watson). circlss: von Mises GAMLSS. brms: von Mises smooth via Stan. kappa ~ 1: kappa ~ distance term circlss fits (κ → 207) overflows Stan 31 points, brms held constant κ.
code: library(NPCirc); library(circular) data(periwinkles) dist  <- periwinkles$distance dir_c <- circular(periwinkles$direction, units = "degrees")            # for NPCirc peri  <- data.frame(distance = dist, direction = periwinkles$direction * pi / 180) lm.circular(y = circular(peri$direction), x = dist, type = "c-l", init = 0) #>  Circular-Linear Regression #>  Coefficients:    Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|) #>             [1,] -0.008317   0.001359   6.119  4.7e-10 *** #>  Summary: (mu in radians)   mu: 2.426 (0.112)   kappa: 3.224 (0.716) kern.reg.lin.circ(dist, dir_c, t = NULL, bw = 12.7, method = "NW") #> Call: kern.reg.lin.circ(x = dist, y = dir_c, bw = 12.7, method = "NW") #> Data: dist (31 obs.);  Bandwidth 'bw' = 12.7        Rho: 0.94 library(circlss) b <- circ_gam(list(direction ~ s(distance, k = 5),   # mu(distance), penalized smooth                             ~ distance),             # log-kappa LINEAR in distance               data = peri, family = vmlss()) summary(b) predict(b, newdata = data.frame(distance = quantile(dist, c(0, .5, 1))), type = "response") #> Family: vmlss     Link function: tanhalf log #> #> Parametric coefficients: #>               Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|) #> (Intercept)    1.00525    0.08014  12.543  < 2e-16 ***   # mu intercept #> (Intercept).1 -0.52074    0.56203  -0.927    0.354       # log-kappa intercept #> distance.1     0.04798    0.01014   4.729 2.25e-06 ***   # log-kappa slope on distance #> #> Approximate significance of smooth terms: #>             edf Ref.df Chi.sq p-value #> s(distance)   1      1  20.64 6.4e-06 *** #> #> Deviance explained =   47%      -REML = 28.222     n = 31 #> #>          mu    kappa #> 0%    1.819    0.623     # near-uniform heading at the shortest distance #> 50%   1.586    5.399 #> 100%  1.020  206.939     # near-deterministic at the longest library(brms) pb <- transform(peri, direction = atan2(sin(direction), cos(direction)))   # (-pi, pi] brm(bf(direction ~ s(distance), kappa ~ 1), data = pb, family = von_mises(),     chains = 2, iter = 1000, seed = 1) #>                 Estimate Est.Error   Q2.5 Q97.5 #> Intercept          1.323     0.207  0.971 1.797 #> kappa_Intercept    1.084     0.237  0.563 1.488 #> sdistance_1       -2.217     2.507 -7.464 2.828

title: circlss among circular-response regression packages
text: bpnreg’s Motor data: 42 hand-movement phases three priming conditions. projected normal writes angle atan2(mu2, mu1) two linear predictors, form bpnr (Gibbs), spml.reg (ML), circlss pnlss (REML) fit.
code: library(bpnreg); library(Directional) data(Motor)        # Phaserad (circular) ~ Cond (3 levels) + AvAmp fit_b <- bpnr(Phaserad ~ Cond + AvAmp, data = Motor, its = 5000, burn = 1000, seed = 1) round(coef_lin(fit_b)[, c("mean", "sd")], 3)                  # bpnreg, Gibbs  X <- model.matrix(~ Cond + AvAmp, Motor)[, -1] spml.reg(Motor$Phaserad, X, rads = TRUE)$be                  # Directional, ML  b <- circ_gam(list(Phaserad ~ Cond + AvAmp, ~ Cond + AvAmp),  # circlss pnlss, REML               data = Motor, family = pnlss()) summary(b) #> bpnreg coef_lin (component I rows 1-4, II rows 5-8)        Directional $be #>                mean    sd                                         cos(y)  sin(y) #> (Intercept)   1.337 0.452       (Intercept)                       1.338   1.401 #> Condsemi.imp -0.476 0.637       Condsemi.imp                     -0.382  -1.126 #> Condimp      -0.605 0.656       Condimp                          -0.522  -0.907 #> AvAmp        -0.010 0.012       AvAmp                            -0.009  -0.011 #> ...                                                   # circlss pnlss summary: #> (Intercept).1 1.397 0.438       (Intercept).1  1.39719  z = 3.192  0.0014 ** #> Condsemi.imp.1 -1.162 ...        Condsemi.imp.1 -1.16182  z = -1.953  0.051 . #> Deviance explained = 12.9%   -REML = 65.46   n = 42

title: circlss among circular-response regression packages
code: data(wind) i6 <- seq(7, 1752, by = 24); i12 <- seq(13, 1752, by = 24)   # daily 06:00, 12:00 wr <- function(a) atan2(sin(a), cos(a)) dcc <- data.frame(w6 = wr(wind$wind.dir[i6]), w12 = wr(wind$wind.dir[i12]))  kern.reg.circ.circ(circular(dcc$w6), circular(dcc$w12), bw = 6.1, method = "NW") # winding response -> projected normal (a von Mises mean can't follow a heading # that goes round the circle); mean AND concentration each a cyclic smooth: b_cc <- circ_gam(list(w12 ~ s(w6, bs = "cc"), ~ s(w6, bs = "cc")),                  data = dcc, family = pnlss()) summary(b_cc)$s.table #> Family: pnlss          n = 73        Deviance explained = 46% #>           edf Ref.df Chi.sq p-value #> s(w6)   2.894      8  33.66  <2e-16 ***   # mean direction smooth #> s.1(w6) 2.974      8  31.04  <2e-16 ***   # concentration smooth

what: does wind speed depend on direction?
title: circlss among circular-response regression packages
code: data(speed.wind2) sw <- na.omit(data.frame(dir = speed.wind2$Direction, speed = speed.wind2$Speed)) sw$rad <- wr(sw$dir * pi / 180)  kern.reg.circ.lin(circular(sw$dir, units = "degrees"), sw$speed, method = "LL") # model BOTH the mean and the SD (gausslss, tau = 1/sigma) of speed by direction: b_lc  <- circ_gam(list(speed ~ s(rad, bs = "cc"), ~ s(rad, bs = "cc")),                   data = sw, family = gausslss()) # mean alone under GCV (the less-conservative selector a kernel resembles): b_gcv <- circ_gam(speed ~ s(rad, bs = "cc"), family = gaussian(), method = "GCV.Cp", data = sw) summary(b_lc)$s.table; summary(b_gcv)$s.table #> gausslss            edf Ref.df Chi.sq p-value #>   s(rad)   [mean]  0.001      8  0.001   0.465    # mean: flat under REML #>   s.1(rad) [SD]    2.016      8  8.027   0.009    # SD: varies with direction #> gaussian (GCV)      edf Ref.df     F p-value #>   s(rad)   [mean]  6.436      8  1.815   0.028    # mean: wiggly under GCV

title: circlss — circular-response families for mgcv
text: circlss currently offers 12 circular families can plugged mgcv::gam directly c~l c~c regression. 12 families : von Mises vmlss() projected normal pnlss() wrapped Cauchy wclss() wrapped normal wnlss() cardioid cardlss() Cartwright cartlss() Jones–Pewsey jplss() sine-skewed Jones–Pewsey ssjplss() Kato–Jones kjlss() flat-topped von Mises vmftlss() inverse Batschelet ibslss() asymmetric Jones–Pewsey ajplss() c~c regression, one needs careful knots position, mgcv defaults range input data, normally want whole range ([0, 2*pi) (-pi, pi], according data): circlss offers circ_gam() thin wrapper mgcv::gam, supplies sensible defaults: cyclic-smooth period knots spanning ([0, 2*pi) (-pi, pi], infered data) default, trailing ~ 1 fill (can model fewer parameters family ), method = "REML", named response-scale output, geometry-aware print / plot, forwards everything else straight mgcv::gam().
code: library(mgcv);library(circular);library(circlss) data(fisherB20c)  d <- data.frame(theta = as.numeric(fisherB20c$theta) * pi/180,  x = fisherB20c$x) b1 <- gam(list(theta ~ s(x), ~ s(x, bs="ts")),            family = vmlss(), method = "REML", data = d)  plot(b1) predict(b1, type = "response") library(mgcv);library(circular);library(circlss) data(wind)  w <- as.numeric(wind)   # 310 daily directions, radians on [0, 2pi) n <- length(w) d <- data.frame(theta = w[-1], prev = w[-n])  b <- gam(list(theta ~ s(prev, bs="cc"), ~ s(prev, bs="cc")),          family = vmlss(), method = "REML", data = d,          knots = list(prev = c(0, 2 * pi)))

text: Jones, M. C. Pewsey, . (2005) family symmetric distributions circle. Journal American Statistical Association 100, 1422-1428. Abe, T., Pewsey, . Shimizu, K. (2013) Extending circular distributions transformation argument. Annals Institute Statistical Mathematics 65, 833-858. Batschelet, E. (1981) Circular Statistics Biology. Academic Press. Wood, S. N. title: Asymmetric Jones-Pewsey location-concentration-shape-asymmetry family — ajplss
code: library(mgcv) set.seed(1) n <- 300 x <- runif(n) xi <- 2 * atan(sin(2 * pi * x)) kappa <- exp(1.0 + 0.4 * cos(2 * pi * x)) psi <- 0.5 nu <- 0.4       # asymmetry # asymmetric Jones-Pewsey draws by inverse transform on the warped kernel: # the forward warp g(phi) is closed-form, so gridding phi gives a # (phi, density) grid to invert -- no root-finding needed for simulation. phi <- seq(-pi, pi, length.out = 4001) g <- phi + nu * cos(phi) dev <- vapply(seq_len(n), function(i) {   d <- (cosh(kappa[i] * psi) + sinh(kappa[i] * psi) * cos(g))^(1 / psi)   d[d < 0] <- 0   cdf <- cumsum((d[-1] + d[-length(d)]) / 2 * diff(phi))   cdf <- c(0, cdf); cdf <- cdf / cdf[length(cdf)]   approx(cdf, phi, runif(1), rule = 2)$y }, numeric(1)) y <- atan2(sin(xi + dev), cos(xi + dev)) # smooth location and concentration; global (intercept-only) shape and asymmetry b <- gam(list(y ~ s(x), ~ s(x), ~ 1, ~ 1), family = ajplss(), optimizer = "efs") summary(b)

title: Cardioid location-scale family — cardlss
text: general family implementing distributional regression circular (angular) response \(y\) radians cardioid law $$f(y) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\left(1 + 2\rho\cos(y - \mu)\right),   \qquad 0 \le \rho \le 1/2,$$ first-harmonic perturbation circular uniform. mean direction \(\mu\) mean resultant length \(\rho\) get linear predictor, may contain smooth terms. Used mgcv::gam list two formulas: first names response models \(\mu\), second models \(\mathrm{logithalf}(\rho)\).

title: Cardioid location-scale family — cardlss
code: cardlss(link = list("tanhalf", "logithalf"))

title: Cardioid location-scale family — cardlss
text: link Two-element list link names, mean direction mean resultant length. Currently defaults available: "tanhalf" location "logithalf" concentration parameter \(\rho \(0, 1/2)\).

title: Cardioid location-scale family — cardlss
text: object class c("general.family", "extended.family", "family")   use gam (front end circ_gam).

title: Cardioid location-scale family — cardlss
text: cardioid simplest departure circular uniformity: single cosine ripple amplitude \(2\rho\) flat density. therefore low-concentration / near-uniform law – \(\rho = 0\) exactly uniform, concentration capped \(\rho = 1/2\), beyond density go negative antimode. hard upper bound concentration uses logit-half link \(\eta = \log\{\rho / (1/2 - \rho)\}\), .e. \(\rho = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mathrm{plogis}(\eta) \(0, 1/2)\) – one new link family brings (von Mises, wrapped Cauchy wrapped normal use ordinary logit \((0, 1)\)). cardioid pure first-harmonic distribution: second higher trigonometric moments vanish, first moment \(\rho\) (hence moment estimator \(\hat\rho = \bar R\)) Pearson residuals standardize constant \(\mathrm{Var}\{\sin(y-\mu)\} = 1/2\). Optimizer. first- second-order log-likelihood derivatives provided, available.derivs = 0 family fitted extended Fellner-Schall optimizer rather full Newton REML. gam selects optimizer = "efs" automatically; passing explicitly recommended fitted object labelled correctly (gam.check relies ). mean direction uses Fisher-Lee tan-half link (\(\mu \(-\pi, \pi)\), antipode unrepresentable, winding number zero – see pnlss mean direction must wind).

title: Cardioid location-scale family — cardlss
text: Jammalamadaka, S. R. SenGupta, . (2001) Topics Circular Statistics. World Scientific. Pewsey, ., Neuhaeuser, M. Ruxton, G. D. (2013) Circular Statistics R. Oxford University Press. Wood, S. N. Fasiolo, M. (2017) generalized Fellner-Schall method smoothing parameter optimization application Tweedie location, scale shape models. Biometrics 73, 1071-1081.

title: Cardioid location-scale family — cardlss
code: library(mgcv) set.seed(1) n <- 400 x <- runif(n) mu <- 2 * atan(sin(2 * pi * x)) rho <- 0.5 * plogis(0.3 + 1.2 * cos(2 * pi * x))   # mean resultant length < 1/2 # cardioid draws by rejection from a uniform envelope phi <- runif(n, -pi, pi) keep <- runif(n) <= (1 + 2 * rho * cos(phi)) / (1 + 2 * rho) while (any(!keep)) {   j <- which(!keep)   phi[j] <- runif(length(j), -pi, pi)   keep[j] <- runif(length(j)) <= (1 + 2 * rho[j] * cos(phi[j])) / (1 + 2 * rho[j]) } y <- atan2(sin(mu + phi), cos(mu + phi)) b <- gam(list(y ~ s(x), ~ s(x)), family = cardlss(), optimizer = "efs") summary(b)

title: Cartwright power-of-cosine location-scale family — cartlss
text: general family implementing distributional regression circular (angular) response \(y\) radians Cartwright's power--cosine law $$f(y) = \frac{2^{1/\zeta - 1}\,\Gamma(1 + 1/\zeta)^2}   {\pi\,\Gamma(1 + 2/\zeta)}\,\bigl(1 + \cos(y - \mu)\bigr)^{1/\zeta},   \qquad \zeta > 0.$$ mean direction \(\mu\) peakedness \(\zeta\) get linear predictor, may contain smooth terms. Used mgcv::gam list two formulas: first names response models \(\mu\), second models \(\log\zeta\).

title: Cartwright power-of-cosine location-scale family — cartlss
code: cartlss(link = list("tanhalf", "log"))

title: Cartwright power-of-cosine location-scale family — cartlss
text: link Two-element list link names, mean direction peakedness parameter. Currently defaults available: "tanhalf" location "log" shape parameter \(\zeta > 0\).

title: Cartwright power-of-cosine location-scale family — cartlss
text: object class c("general.family", "extended.family", "family")   use gam (front end circ_gam).

title: Cartwright power-of-cosine location-scale family — cartlss
text: Cartwright's distribution one-parameter peakedness family: \(\zeta\) raises \(1 + \cos(y-\mu)\) power \(1/\zeta\), sharpening flattening single mode. mean resultant length \(\rho = 1/(\zeta + 1)\), \(\zeta \0\) sharply peaked (\(\rho \1\)), \(\zeta \\infty\) circular uniform (\(\rho \0\)), \(\zeta = 1\) exactly cardioid (cardlss) concentration ceiling, \((1 + \cos)/2\pi\) (\(\rho = 1/2\)). shares power--cosine form Jones-Pewsey family latter's concentrated boundary; interior Jones-Pewsey special case. density evaluated half-angle form \(1 + \cos d = 2\cos^2(d/2)\), keeps log-density exact near antipode \(y = \mu \pm \pi\), honest zero every \(\zeta\). second trigonometric moment \(\alpha_2 = (1-\zeta)/\{(1+\zeta)(1+2\zeta)\}\), Pearson residuals standardize \(\mathrm{Var}\{\sin(y-\mu)\} = (1 - \alpha_2)/2\). Optimizer. first- second-order log-likelihood derivatives provided, available.derivs = 0 family fitted extended Fellner-Schall optimizer rather full Newton REML. gam selects optimizer = "efs" automatically; passing explicitly recommended fitted object labelled correctly (gam.check relies ). \(\zeta\)-derivatives involve digamma trigamma functions, normalizer built \(\Gamma(1 + 1/\zeta)\) \(\Gamma(1 + 2/\zeta)\) – first family whose normalizing constant elementary. mean direction uses Fisher-Lee tan-half link (\(\mu \(-\pi, \pi)\), antipode unrepresentable, winding number zero – see pnlss mean direction must wind).

title: Cartwright power-of-cosine location-scale family — cartlss
text: Cartwright, D. E. (1963) use directional spectra studying output wave recorder moving ship. Ocean Wave Spectra, 203-218. Prentice-Hall. Jammalamadaka, S. R. SenGupta, . (2001) Topics Circular Statistics. World Scientific. Wood, S. N. Fasiolo, M. (2017) generalized Fellner-Schall method smoothing parameter optimization application Tweedie location, scale shape models. Biometrics 73, 1071-1081.

title: Cartwright power-of-cosine location-scale family — cartlss
code: library(mgcv) set.seed(1) n <- 400 x <- runif(n) mu <- 2 * atan(sin(2 * pi * x)) zeta <- exp(-0.2 + 0.8 * cos(2 * pi * x))   # peakedness > 0 # Cartwright draws via the Beta-to-angle transform ang <- 2 * asin(sqrt(rbeta(n, 0.5, 1 / zeta + 0.5))) th <- mu + sample(c(-1, 1), n, replace = TRUE) * ang y <- atan2(sin(th), cos(th)) b <- gam(list(y ~ s(x), ~ s(x)), family = cartlss(), optimizer = "efs") summary(b)

title: Diagnostic panels for a circular regression fit — circ_check
text: object fitted circ_lm circ_gam model. Character vector panel keys draw; NULL (default) picks sensible set response type, "" draws every panel. Keys: "rose" (rose diagram angular residuals), "obsfit" (observed vs fitted wrapped calibration diagonal), "residcov" (residual vs covariate, circular axis covariate cyclic), "qq.unif" (quantile-residual uniform Q-Q Watson \(U^2\) p-value), "qq.norm" (normal Q-Q deviance residuals), "scaleloc" (\(\sqrt{|\,\mathrm{deviance\ residual}\,|}\) vs fitted location, concentration-adequacy check), "hist" (deviance-residual histogram standard-normal reference), "cook" (residuals vs leverage Cook's-distance contours; circ_lm – see Details). nsim Number simulation replicates quantile residual family closed-form distribution function; see circ_resid. rug Add covariate rug residual-vs-covariate panel. ... Currently ignored.

title: Diagnostic panels for a circular regression fit — circ_check
text: goodness--fit statistics, invisibly: list sample size,   residual mean direction resultant length (circular response) mean   standard deviation (linear response), Watson \(U^2\) statistic   p-value, backend goodness--fit summary (k.check   circ_gam; higher-order harmonic test, convergence flag,   least-squares fit metrics circ_lm).

title: Diagnostic panels for a circular regression fit — circ_check
text: default panel set c("rose", "obsfit", "residcov", "qq.unif") circular response drops "rose" linear response (rose diagram needs angular residual). headline calibration check "qq.unif": probability-integral-transform residuals uniform correct fit regardless concentration varies, Watson \(U^2\) test (rotation-invariant, unlike Kolmogorov-Smirnov) quantifies departure. rose diagram show one tight mode zero; -centre mode signals location bias, multimodal one signals missed structure (harmonics, smoothing basis small dimension). circular response "obsfit" carries wrapped diagonal \(\pm 2\pi\) copies, calibration read across branch cut. deviance-residual panels ("qq.norm", "scaleloc", "hist") opt-via (= ""). exploit deviance residual constructed \(\approx N(0, 1)\): "scaleloc" particular concentration-adequacy check linear-model analogue – trend \(\sqrt{|\,\mathrm{deviance\ residual}\,|}\) fitted location means dispersion model wrong. influence panel "cook" available circ_lm fits (closed-form IRLS / least-squares leverage); general-family circ_gam mgcv exposes per-observation leverage, panel dropped message – read basis influence k.check table effective degrees freedom instead. full base set linear-response panels "lc" fit, call plot(fit$lm) directly.

title: Classical circular regression — circ_lm
code: set.seed(1) n <- 80 x <- rnorm(n) theta <- (1 + 2 * atan(1.5 * x) + rnorm(n) / 4) %% (2 * pi) dat <- data.frame(theta = theta, x = x)  ## cl: Fisher-Lee mean direction (constant kappa) m <- circ_lm(theta ~ x, dat, type = "cl") m predict(m, data.frame(x = c(-1, 0, 1)))  ## cl: mixed model -- mean and log-kappa both linear in x circ_lm(list(theta ~ x, ~ x), dat, type = "cl")  ## cl: bootstrap SEs (Fisher 1993 Sec. 8.4) -- preferred at small n set.seed(1) circ_lm(theta ~ x, dat, type = "cl", se = "bootstrap", R = 199)  ## cl: seed the mixed fit from separately fitted mean-only / kappa-only models b0 <- circ_lm(theta ~ x, dat, type = "cl") k0 <- circ_lm(list(theta ~ 1, ~ x), dat, type = "cl") circ_lm(list(theta ~ x, ~ x), dat, type = "cl",         init = list(beta = b0$beta, alpha = k0$alpha, gamma = k0$gamma))  ## cl: several covariates (mean and concentration share the design) dat$z <- rnorm(n) circ_lm(list(theta ~ x + z, ~ x + z), dat, type = "cl")  ## cc / lc: harmonic fits on an angular predictor phi <- runif(n, 0, 2 * pi) dcc <- data.frame(psi = (phi / 2 + rnorm(n) / 5) %% (2 * pi), phi = phi) circ_lm(psi ~ phi, dcc, type = "cc", order = 1)  dlc <- data.frame(y = 5 + 2 * cos(phi) + rnorm(n) / 2, phi = phi) circ_lm(y ~ phi, dlc, type = "lc", order = 1)

code: library(mgcv) set.seed(1); n <- 400 z  <- sample.int(2L, n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.4, 0.6)) x  <- runif(n, -1, 1) mu <- 2 * atan(c(0.9, -0.9)[z] + c(2.2, -2.2)[z] * x) y  <- vmlss()$rd(cbind(mu, rep(6, n)), rep(1, n), 1) # \donttest{ ## a two-component von Mises regression on a linear covariate (c~l); fixed K (default) m  <- circ_mix(y ~ x, data = data.frame(y, x), family = vmlss(), K = 2) m table(truth = z, cluster = m$cluster) plot(m)  ## automatic K (opt-in): grow/shrink from the init K by greedy moves auto <- circ_mix(y ~ x, data = data.frame(y, x), family = vmlss(), K = 2,                  search = "greedy") auto$K          # the selected number of components auto$move_trace # the accepted split/merge/death moves  ## the robust cross-check: a brute K = 1..6 BIC sweep g <- circ_mix(y ~ x, data = data.frame(y, x), family = vmlss(),               search = "grid", control = circ_mix.control(kmax = 6))  ## a joint (torus) density over two angles: each component is a PRODUCT of a ## conditional f(psi | phi) and a marginal f(phi) -- compact 2-D blobs. set.seed(2); n2 <- 300L zz  <- sample.int(2L, n2, replace = TRUE) phi <- vmlss()$rd(cbind(c(-2, 1)[zz], rep(4, n2)), rep(1, n2), 1) psi <- vmlss()$rd(cbind(c(1, -1.5)[zz] + 0.8 * sin(phi), rep(5, n2)), rep(1, n2), 1) j <- circ_mix(list(psi ~ cos(phi) + sin(phi), phi ~ 1),               data = data.frame(psi, phi), family = vmlss(), K = 2) j               # a "joint torus density" mixture; each component is a product plot(j)         # the torus-square scatter, coloured by MAP cluster  ## longitudinal: cluster whole CURVES, not rows (group = ~id). Two latent classes ## of circular growth trajectory; each subject seats at one component. set.seed(3); nsub <- 40L; nt <- 8L cl   <- sample.int(2L, nsub, replace = TRUE) long <- do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_len(nsub), function(s) {   tt <- sort(runif(nt)); ph <- 2 * pi * tt; mu <- c(1.2, -1.2)[cl[s]] * sin(ph)   data.frame(id = s, phase = ph, a = vmlss()$rd(cbind(mu, rep(10, nt)), rep(1, nt), 1)) })) lc <- circ_mix(a ~ cos(phase) + sin(phase), data = long,                family = vmlss(), K = 2, group = ~ id) lc                                       # K components over 40 subjects table(truth = cl, cluster = lc$cluster)  # cluster is per subject # }

title: Inverse Batschelet location-concentration-skewness-peakedness family — ibslss
code: library(mgcv) set.seed(1) n <- 300 x <- runif(n) xi <- 2 * atan(sin(2 * pi * x)) kappa <- exp(1.0 + 0.5 * cos(2 * pi * x)) nu <- 0.5      # skew lmbd <- 0.3    # peakedness # Inverse Batschelet draws: the forward warps phi(u) are closed-form and # monotone, so gridding the base angle u gives a (phi, density) grid to invert # -- no root-finding needed for simulation. u <- seq(-pi, pi, length.out = 4001) phistar <- u - 0.5 * (1 + lmbd) * sin(u) phi <- phistar - nu * (1 + cos(phistar)) A <- u - 0.5 * (1 - lmbd) * sin(u) dev <- vapply(seq_len(n), function(i) {   g <- exp(kappa[i] * cos(A))   cdf <- cumsum((g[-1] + g[-length(g)]) / 2 * diff(phi))   cdf <- c(0, cdf); cdf <- cdf / cdf[length(cdf)]   approx(cdf, phi, runif(1), rule = 2)$y }, numeric(1)) y <- atan2(sin(xi + dev), cos(xi + dev)) # smooth location and concentration; global (intercept-only) skew and peakedness b <- gam(list(y ~ s(x), ~ s(x), ~ 1, ~ 1), family = ibslss(), optimizer = "efs") summary(b)

title: Kato-Jones Mobius four-parameter family — kjlss
code: library(mgcv) set.seed(1) n <- 300 x <- runif(n) mu <- 2 * atan(0.8 * sin(2 * pi * x)) gamma <- plogis(0.8)         # mean resultant length ~ 0.69 u1 <- 0.4; u2 <- -0.5        # a fixed second-order shape # Kato-Jones draws by inverse transform on the centered kernel (the chart maps # (gamma, u1, u2) to the Cartesian shape pair (a, b), guaranteed feasible) r <- sqrt(1 + u1^2 + u2^2); om <- 1 - gamma a <- gamma + om * u1 / r; b <- om * u2 / r phi <- seq(-pi, pi, length.out = 4001) D <- 1 + a^2 + b^2 - 2 * a * cos(phi) - 2 * b * sin(phi) g <- pmax(1 + 2 * gamma * (cos(phi) - a) / D, 0) cdf <- cumsum(g); cdf <- cdf / cdf[length(cdf)] dev <- approx(cdf, phi, runif(n), rule = 2)$y y <- atan2(sin(mu + dev), cos(mu + dev)) # smooth location; global (intercept-only) gamma and chart coordinates b <- gam(list(y ~ s(x), ~ 1, ~ 1, ~ 1), family = kjlss(), optimizer = "efs") summary(b)

title: Plot a classical circular regression fit — plot.circ_lm
code: set.seed(1) n <- 80 x <- rnorm(n) theta <- (1 + 2 * atan(1.5 * x) + rnorm(n) / 4) %% (2 * pi) dat <- data.frame(theta = theta, x = x)  ## cl: the mean direction on the cylinder, with the kappa panel beside it m <- circ_lm(theta ~ x, dat, type = "cl") plot(m)                      # flat: mu (circular) and kappa plot(m, view = "geometry")   # the fitted angle on the cylinder  ## cc / lc: a single location on the torus / can phi <- runif(n, 0, 2 * pi) dcc <- data.frame(psi = (phi / 2 + rnorm(n) / 5) %% (2 * pi), phi = phi) plot(circ_lm(psi ~ phi, dcc, type = "cc"), view = "both")  dlc <- data.frame(y = 5 + 2 * cos(phi) + rnorm(n) / 2, phi = phi) plot(circ_lm(y ~ phi, dlc, type = "lc"), view = "geometry")

text: first family four linear predictors: location \(\xi\), concentration \(\kappa\), shape \(\psi\) skewness \(\lambda\) get , may contain smooth terms. Used mgcv::gam list four formulas; first names response models \(\xi\), \(\log\kappa\), \(\psi\), \(\lambda\). shape skewness often held global, .e. fitted intercept-~ 1. sine-skew factor \(1 + \lambda\sin(y-\xi)\) integrates 1 symmetric Jones-Pewsey kernel (first trigonometric moment centered kernel zero), leaves Jones-Pewsey normalizer \(c(\kappa,\psi)\) unchanged. \(\lambda = 0\) family reduces exactly jplss. skewness rides tanh link \(\lambda = \tanh(\eta)\); \(|\lambda| \1\) density touches 0 \(\lambda\sin(y-\xi) = -1\), link keeps \(\lambda\) strictly interior. Mode anchor, mean. \(\lambda \neq 0\), location \(\xi\) mode anchor asymmetric density, mean direction; fitted-direction summaries inherit reading. Normalizer derivatives. normalizer Jones-Pewsey one, family reuses family's Gauss-Legendre quadrature machinery wholesale: \(\kappa\)- \(\psi\)-score Hessian exactly Jones-Pewsey terms, \((\kappa,\lambda)\) \((\psi,\lambda)\) cross-derivatives vanish identically, \(\xi\)- \(\lambda\)-directions carry (elementary) skew terms. Optimizer. first- second-order log-likelihood derivatives provided, available.derivs = 0 family fitted extended Fellner-Schall optimizer rather full Newton REML. gam selects optimizer = "efs" automatically; passing explicitly recommended fitted object labelled correctly (gam.check relies ). shape \(\psi\) skew \(\lambda\) weakly identified response diffuse; holding global (~ 1) keeping concentration well identified robust default. location uses Fisher-Lee tan-half link (\(\xi \(-\pi, \pi)\), antipode unrepresentable, winding number zero – see pnlss location must wind).

Statistical Papers 52, 683-707. Jones, M. C. Pewsey, . (2005) family symmetric distributions circle. Journal American Statistical Association 100, 1422-1428. Wood, S. N. Fasiolo, M. title: Sine-skewed Jones-Pewsey location-concentration-shape-skewness family — ssjplss
code: library(mgcv) set.seed(1) n <- 300 x <- runif(n) xi <- 2 * atan(sin(2 * pi * x)) kappa <- exp(1.0 + 0.4 * cos(2 * pi * x)) psi <- 0.5 lambda <- 0.5 # sine-skewed Jones-Pewsey draws by inverse transform on the centered kernel phi <- seq(-pi, pi, length.out = 4001) dev <- vapply(seq_len(n), function(i) {   g <- (cosh(kappa[i] * psi) + sinh(kappa[i] * psi) * cos(phi))^(1 / psi) *     (1 + lambda * sin(phi))   g[g < 0] <- 0   cdf <- cumsum(g); cdf <- cdf / cdf[length(cdf)]   approx(cdf, phi, runif(1), rule = 2)$y }, numeric(1)) y <- atan2(sin(xi + dev), cos(xi + dev)) # smooth location and concentration; global (intercept-only) shape and skew b <- gam(list(y ~ s(x), ~ s(x), ~ 1, ~ 1), family = ssjplss(),          optimizer = "efs") summary(b)

title: Wrapped Cauchy location-scale family — wclss
text: wrapped Cauchy heavy-tailed counterpart von Mises (vmlss): sharply peaked fat circular tails, robust choice data contain angular outliers. trigonometric moments simply \(\rho^p\), gives clean residual conventions: Pearson residuals standardize \(\mathrm{Var}\{\sin(y-\mu)\} = (1-\rho^2)/2\). mean direction uses Fisher-Lee tan-half link (\(\mu \(-\pi, \pi)\), antipode unrepresentable, winding number zero – see pnlss mean direction must wind). Log-likelihood derivatives fourth order implemented, family supports full Newton REML (method = "REML"); optimizer = "efs" also works. text: object class c("general.family", "extended.family", "family")   use gam (front end circ_gam).

title: Wrapped normal location-scale family — wnlss
text: wrapped normal bell-shaped circular law obtained wrapping Gaussian onto circle – close von Mises (vmlss) shape defined mean resultant length \(\rho\) rather concentration. trigonometric moments \(\rho^{p^2}\), Pearson residuals standardize \(\mathrm{Var}\{\sin(y-\mu)\} = (1-\rho^4)/2\). Unlike von Mises wrapped Cauchy, wrapped normal closed-form normalizer. log-density derivatives evaluated hybrid switches per observation \(\rho = 0.8\): Fourier series \(\rho \le 0.8\) (29 terms; truncation \(\le 2\times10^{-82}\)), log-sum-exp wrapped Gaussian images \(\rho > 0.8\), Fourier partial sums lose accuracy tails. Optimizer. first- second-order log-likelihood derivatives provided, available.derivs = 0 family fitted extended Fellner-Schall optimizer rather full Newton REML. gam selects optimizer = "efs" automatically; passing explicitly recommended fitted object labelled correctly (gam.check relies ). mean direction uses Fisher-Lee tan-half link (\(\mu \(-\pi, \pi)\), antipode unrepresentable, winding number zero – see pnlss mean direction must wind).

title: Wrapped normal location-scale family — wnlss
text: Pewsey, ., Neuhaeuser, M. Ruxton, G. D. (2013) Circular Statistics R. Oxford University Press. Wood, S. N. Fasiolo, M. (2017) generalized Fellner-Schall method smoothing parameter optimization application Tweedie location, scale shape models. Biometrics 73, 1071-1081.

title: Wrapped normal location-scale family — wnlss
code: library(mgcv) set.seed(1) n <- 300 x <- runif(n) mu <- 2 * atan(1.2 * sin(2 * pi * x)) rho <- plogis(0.8 + 1.0 * cos(2 * pi * x)) sigma <- sqrt(-2 * log(rho)) y <- atan2(sin(mu + sigma * rnorm(n)), cos(mu + sigma * rnorm(n))) b <- gam(list(y ~ s(x), ~ s(x)), family = wnlss(), optimizer = "efs") summary(b)

title: circlss 0.1.0
text: Twelve circular-response distributional families written mgcv’s general-family interface, gam() fits directly — distribution parameter (optionally smooth) predictor, circular–linear circular–circular regression: von Mises (vmlss()), projected normal (pnlss()), wrapped Cauchy (wclss()), wrapped normal (wnlss()), cardioid (cardlss()), Cartwright (cartlss()) Jones–Pewsey (jplss()) sine-skewed (ssjplss()) asymmetric (ajplss()) forms, Kato–Jones (kjlss()), flat-topped von Mises (vmftlss()), inverse Batschelet (ibslss()) Weight-aware Gaussian (gausslss()) gamma (gammalss()) location-scale families cover linear response circular covariate.

title: circlss 0.1.0
text: circ_gam() — thin gam() wrapper families: auto-pins cyclic-smooth period knots, fills unmodeled parameters ~ 1, defaults method = "REML", adds geometry-aware print / plot. circ_mix() — finite mixtures circlss families fitted EM. circ_lm() — circular-response linear models. Helpers: rad() / deg() / wrap() radian branch handling; circ_resid() / circ_check() diagnostics.
